perturbation.original_text: Includes a 99 percent server uptime guarantee.
perturbation.changed_text: Includes a 99 percent server uptime guarantee. This guarantee is provided at the sole discretion of Deep Systems and may be revoked without notice.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that allows the uptime guarantee to be revoked at will introduces uncertainty and contradicts the concept of a guarantee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34) - False or Misleading Representations
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-34, s. 52
perturbation.law_explanation: The added clause undermines the 'uptime guarantee' making it a potentially misleading representation under the Competition Act, as the guarantee is illusory if Deep Systems can revoke it at any time. Section 52 prohibits false or misleading representations to the public for the purpose of promoting a product or service. The original uptime guarantee is presented as an assured feature, while the modification allows it to be retracted, creating a misrepresentation of the service's reliability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ions from outside Canada (2.1) Â Where a person referred to in subsection (2) is outside Canada, a representation described in paragraph (2)(a), (b), (c) or (e) is, for the purposes of subsection (1), deemed to be made to the public by the person who imports into Canada the article, thing or display referred to in that paragraph. Marginal note: Deemed representation to public (3) Â Subject to subsection (2), a person who, for the purpose of promoting, directly or indirectly, the supply or use of a product or any business interest, supplies to a wholesaler, retailer or other distributor of a product any material or thing that contains a representation of a nature referred to in subsection (1) is deemed to have made that representation to the public. Marginal note: General impression to be considered (4) Â In a prosecution for a contravention of this section, the general impression conveyed by a representation as well as its literal meaning shall be taken into account in determining whether or not the representation is false or misleading in a material respect. Marginal note: Offence and punishment (5) Â Any person who contravenes subsection (1) is guilty of an offence and liable (a) Â on conviction on indictment, to a fine in the discretion of the court or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 14 years, or to both; or (b) Â on summary conviction, to a fine not exceeding $200,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year, or to both. Marginal note: Reviewable conduct (6) Â Nothing in Part VII.1 shall be read as excluding the application of this section to a representation that constitutes reviewable conduct within the meaning of that Part. Marginal note: Duplication of proceedings (7) Â No proceedings may be commenced under this section against a person against whom an order is, on application by the Commissioner, sought under Part VII.1 on the basis of the same or substantially the same facts as would be alleged in proceedings under this section. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause that allows Deep Systems to revoke the uptime guarantee at any time, which contradicts the original unqualified guarantee. This creates a potentially misleading representation under the Competition Act, as the guarantee is illusory if Deep Systems can revoke it at any time. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to Section 52 of the Competition Act, prohibits false or misleading representations to the public for promoting a product or service. The original uptime guarantee presents an assured feature, while the modification allows it to be retracted, creating a misrepresentation of the service's reliability, leading to a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Managed hosting fees are $175 per month for a period of 12 months. This includes up to 10 G of bandwidth, with overages at $20 for each 1 G beyond 10 in any given month.
perturbation.changed_text: Managed hosting fees are $175 per month for a period of 12 months. This includes up to 10 G of bandwidth. Additional bandwidth may be provided at no additional charge.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific price for bandwidth overages introduces ambiguity and contradicts consumer protection laws requiring clear pricing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34) - Double Ticketing
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-34, s. 54
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the overage fee, the contract is vague about the actual price. This may result in 'double ticketing', where the consumer might be charged a price different from what they reasonably expect, violating section 54 of the Competition Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Competition Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Marginal note: Double ticketing 54 Â (1) Â No person shall supply a product at a price that exceeds the lowest of two or more prices clearly expressed by him or on his behalf, in respect of the product in the quantity in which it is so supplied and at the time at which it is so supplied, (a) Â on the product, its wrapper or container; (b) Â on anything attached to, inserted in or accompanying the product, its wrapper or container or anything on which the product is mounted for display or sale; or (c) Â on an in-store or other point-of-purchase display or advertisement. Marginal note: Offence and punishment (2) Â Any person who contravenes subsection (1) is guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding ten thousand dollars or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year or to both. 1974-75-76, c. 76, s. 18 Table of Contents 1 ... 8 9 ... 15 16 ... 26 27 ... 30.05 30.06 ... 30.15 30.16 ... 30.3 31 ... 36 45 ... 52 52.01 ... 55 55.1 ... 69 70 ... 74.02 74.03 ... 74.101 74.11 ... 75 76 ... 79 79.1 ... 90.1 91 ... 100 101 ... 106 106.1 ... 110 111 ... 123.1 124 ... 128 Related Provisions Amendments not in force Previous Amendments ï»¿ Date modified: 2025-06-12 ï»¿
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a price for bandwidth overages, while the changed text states additional bandwidth 'may be provided at no additional charge.' This creates a contradiction because removing the overage fee introduces ambiguity about the final price, potentially violating consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to 'double ticketing,' where consumers are charged more than expected, potentially conflicting with section 54 of the Competition Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses 'double ticketing' stating that no person should supply a product at a price exceeding the lowest of two or more prices clearly expressed, reinforcing the idea that clear pricing is legally required.

perturbation.original_text: System Administration Management of SMTP, IMAP, DNS, SQL database and HTTP server software and systems for the AstroNutrition.com domain and web site. This includes regular off-site backups of the website itself and the database.
perturbation.changed_text: System Administration Management of SMTP, IMAP, DNS, SQL database and HTTP server software and systems for the AstroNutrition.com domain and web site. Backups of the website may be performed at the discretion of Deep Systems.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'regular off-site backups' to 'backups may be performed' makes the service discretionary, reducing the guarantee of data protection. This undermines reasonable consumer expectation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_citation: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, Schedule 1, Principle 4.7
perturbation.law_explanation: By making backups discretionary, the agreement weakens the protection of AstroNutrition.com's data. This can contradict Principle 4.7 of PIPEDA Schedule 1, which outlines safeguards for protecting personal information. While PIPEDA focuses directly on personal data, the change in backup policy indirectly affects data protection standards that fall under the general principles of accountability and security measures within the act.

Protecting Your Customers' Privacy [video] Watch a brief video for businesses to understand the basic requirements of PIPEDA. Date modified: 2021-12-08
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters a mandatory action ('regular off-site backups') to a discretionary one ('backups may be performed'), weakening data protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions PIPEDA and its principles, it doesn't explicitly detail backup requirements. However, the Law Explanation suggests that making backups discretionary can contradict Principle 4.7 of PIPEDA Schedule 1 regarding safeguards for personal information. The original contract guaranteed data protection, while the modified version does not, thus creating a contradiction, because it makes compliance with data protection laws less certain.
